title: Strange New World: Lecture 4
speaker: Dr. Carl Trueman
source_files: WRS 2021 Ron Taber Lecture Series_ Strange New Worlds - Carl Trueman 4 or 4.txt
outline: Summary of the strange new world
The church's identity in an age of identity politics
Holding firm to a biblical anthropology
Practical wisdom for Christians in the modern world
Conclusion: Hope for the church in a strange new world
summary: Dr. Carl Trueman concludes the four-lecture series, wrapping up the analysis of the strange new world and offering guidance for how the church should respond to the challenges of expressive individualism and the sexual revolution while remaining faithful to Scripture.
transcript:
## Introduction: Possible Responses

I have not solved all the problems. I want to offer some possible responses. The previous lectures have described how the social imaginary — the way people intuitively think about the world — has been shaped by forces over which no single person or organization exerts decisive control. It is therefore impossible to come up with a simple solution.

We also need to accept as churches that the days when the social imaginary was broadly shaped by Christian intuitions are rapidly vanishing. Those of us whose imaginations are formed by Christianity will find ourselves in an increasingly strange and alien environment. And the capacity to retreat from these challenges is rapidly diminishing. Nearly every church I speak at has been touched by LGBT-related questions in its life — in the lives of families, young people, or people in the congregation. If your children have smartphones, this is not something that can be avoided.

## First Response: Acknowledge Our Own Complicity

Before anything else, the church needs to recognize its own complicity in the broader culture.

I mentioned in an earlier lecture that when Rousseau was writing, so were Jonathan Edwards and John Wesley. That is to say, the inner space is biblically and theologically important. But there are problems with the way that inner space has been handled in much of evangelical culture. The church has not been immune.

Think of the problem of religious freedom: not that I think religious freedom is a bad thing — it is far better than its alternatives — but it has brought some tendencies that are problematic. The transformation of the church into a consumer item, the transformation of the Sunday service into an experience designed to make people feel good rather than to encounter the living God, the tendency to choose a church based on what suits us rather than on theological conviction. Rieff — an important sociological thinker — said that in the Middle Ages people went to church not to be made to feel happy but to have their misery explained to them. There is a word of challenge there.

I also think the church has often been vocally critical of sexual immorality outside traditional marriage while being virtually silent on the epidemic of no-fault divorce within the church. The rates of divorce among church members in many traditions are almost identical to the rates in wider society. Without wanting to be unduly harsh, that inconsistency is noticed, and it weakens our credibility. The church needs to repent in these areas. And that awareness of our own complicity should cultivate humility in how we engage with those with whom we disagree.

## Second Response: Learn from History

As the position of the church in Western culture changes dramatically, we see Christians reaching for historical analogies. Some are attracted to theonomy and reconstruction — looking back to the Reformation era, when Reformers really did reshape the broader culture. Some Catholics are drawn to integralism, looking back to the medieval period.

I am sympathetic to learning from these periods, but I do not think we can simply transplant them to today. We are not living in the sixteenth century or the thirteenth century. I think the second century offers more useful analogies.

In the second century, the church was a poorly understood, despised, marginal body. It was working against a culture that had never known Christianity — unlike us, who are working against a culture that has known Christianity and is in the process of *de-Christianizing*. So there are significant differences. But there are also significant similarities:

First, Christianity was little understood and largely viewed with suspicion, just as the faithful Christian church is today. Second, Christians were seen as immoral — teaching things harmful to the common good, specifically in the area of sexuality. Third, Christians were seen as seditious. "Jesus is Lord" was a claim that ran directly against "Caesar is Lord," just as Christian teaching on sexuality today is seen not merely as wrong but as damaging to society and subversive of basic values.

What can we learn from the second-century church's response? Several things.

## Third Response: Be a Community

The second-century church was characterized by genuine community — caring for one another materially and spiritually in a society where such care was otherwise unavailable. As the *Didache* and other early texts make clear, the church was not just a teaching institution; it was a community that organized itself to care for people.

Think back to what I said about freedom and belonging. Identity is formed in community. The strongest identities people have are derived from the strongest relationships they have. Family ties, close friendships — these give us our deepest sense of who we are. The church needs to be a strong community.

If the problem we face with the LGBTQ movement is not just a moral problem — "these people are doing things we disapprove of" — but fundamentally a problem of *identity* — these people are seeking a community in which they belong and are valued — then the solution cannot simply be to teach people why certain behaviors are wrong. It has to be: "This is the true community to which you belong." Jesus himself said: "By this all men will know that you are my disciples, if you love one another." Community is a powerful witness to the truth of the gospel.

The LGBTQ movement has built extraordinarily strong communities. Many people who have come out of that movement describe the community as one of the most compelling things about it — the sense of being accepted, known, and valued. The church ought to be saying: we have a community that is deeper than that. We have a fellowship grounded in the resurrection of Jesus Christ.

Community will look different in different contexts. In a rural setting it will look different from community in a large city. But there is a commonality: gather together, call on the Lord, pray, sing his praise, hear the Word read and preached, celebrate baptism and the Lord's Supper, serve one another and give to the church's work. Those things constitute our community. And beyond the Sunday service, we need to be proactive about friendship and fellowship — hospitality, having people in our homes, pursuing the informal relationships that build genuine community.

The church does not merely engage the culture. The church *is* a culture. One of my colleagues puts it this way: the church does not engage culture — the church is a culture. What we need to present to the world is not merely an argument about why their positions are wrong, but an alternative culture: a community that shows what it truly means to be human, living according to the pattern for which we were made.

## Fourth Response: Teach the Whole Counsel of God

One of the temptations in times of rapid cultural flux is to become so preoccupied with the issues of the moment that we lose sight of the larger mandate: to teach the whole counsel of God. Why do I think transgenderism is wrong? Because I have a comprehensive understanding of what reality is — a grand scheme grounded in the whole of Scripture and Christian theology — and it is that comprehensive framework that allows me to address this particular issue in context.

Some years ago there was a proposal for a study committee to produce a statement on same-sex marriage. There may be a place for such things to help pastors. But the Westminster Standards are already quite clear on what marriage is, and they effectively exclude all alternatives. What we need is not a kind of theological whack-a-mole — constantly addressing the latest issue as it emerges. By the time a thirty-year study committee reports, who knows what we will be addressing? Rather, we need to teach people the whole counsel of God so that they can address specific problems themselves.

This is why the confessions are so useful. They have spoken to generation after generation because they have stood the test of time — there is a reason for that. For those who do not adhere to a confessional tradition, I think you might still find it helpful to have some doctrinal standard that tries to cover the whole counsel of God in a pedagogically structured way.

I do not teach a course on sexuality at the college where I work. I teach a course on creation and anthropology. Because equipping people to think about sexuality requires giving them the background of God's broader plan and purpose — the whole canvas, not just the detail.

## Fifth Response: Shape the Imagination Through Worship

If the problem is the way we instinctively think and feel about the world — if expressive individualism has shaped our intuitions so that we *experience* ourselves as sovereign individuals — then the answer is not only intellectual. Intuitions need to be shaped. And the primary place where Christian intuitions are formed is in worship.

I am a fan of psalm-singing — not exclusively, and certainly not in a way that condemns those in other traditions. But I am a fan of psalm-singing because I think the Psalms shoot straight to the heart. They present the full range of the inner life — joy, grief, anger, confusion, doubt — and they always draw that inner life into conformity with the Word and purposes of God. The Psalms keep theological and emotional perspective on the events of the present. We are creatures of emotion and sentiment, and we need songs of redemption to calibrate those emotions, to set them in their proper context.

Poetry and music together are an extraordinarily powerful combination. The great works of art and literature — Wagner's *Ring Cycle*, for example — do things that prose argument cannot do. They reach the imagination in ways that rational discourse cannot. The Romantics understood this, and they used art to transform the social imaginary. The church should take the power of art and music in worship more seriously.

## Sixth Response: Recover Natural Law

This is perhaps a more controversial point, but I stand by it. We need to think more carefully about natural law arguments — not as a replacement for Scripture, but as a complement to it, particularly in conversations with those who do not accept biblical authority.

Good Christian students who have been taught that homosexuality is forbidden by Scripture sometimes still struggle with the question: *why* does God forbid it? Is it just an arbitrary divine preference? Is it just because the Bible says so? Natural law helps at this point: it enables us to say that the Bible says what it says not arbitrarily, but because this is the structure of the world God created, reflecting the character of God himself. If you go against it, you get what the evidence shows — that this does not constitute a flourishing life. That is not the only argument, but it is an additional one that can help students who accept the biblical teaching but still want to understand why.

## Seventh Response: Hope Rather Than Optimism or Despair

My final point draws on a distinction Rod Dreher has used: the difference between optimism, despair, and hope.

Optimism is the Pollyanna view — everything will be fine, things will work out, someone will turn up to save the day. That is not a Christian stance. Christians who look honestly at the world in which we now live know that the social imaginary is not Christian, and that this is changing rapidly in a direction unfavorable to Christian faith.

Despair is the opposite error: concluding that there is nothing to be done, that the battle is lost.

Hope is neither. Hope is the conviction that the real meaning of my life — and of all life — is not found in the here and now, but in the hereafter. What we're facing in the United States is something we should resist. But we are not permitted to despair, because despair is passing. It is passing because it fails to reckon with the promises of God. Whatever suffering we experience in the present moment — and it may be terrible, even unbearable — it never means that we are severed from the life of the resurrection, the future secured for us by the Lord Jesus Christ.

The Nonconformists of nineteenth-century England are a useful illustration. If you were not Anglican in England in the mid-nineteenth century — if you were Catholic, or Dissenter, or Presbyterian — you could not hold civil office, you could not attend Oxford or Cambridge, you could not access many professions. You could not do much. What could you do? You could be a strong, marginalized community. You could make things. And they made great things — in business, in education, in culture. The Nonconformist tradition built the backbone of industrial Britain.

The marginalization of our generation may feel catastrophic. But it can become an opportunity. The church can become a powerful, genuine community that communicates the gospel in a winsome and compelling way to the next generation, and the generation after that. That is an opportunity for which we should be grateful.

## Questions and Answers

**Q: How should parents teach children about sexuality in this cultural moment?**

First, and most importantly: mitigate the influence of smartphones, YouTube, and TikTok. No child needs unsupervised access to a smartphone. This is the single most important practical step. Second, have these conversations — but in the context of the whole character of God and his plan for human sexuality. Use good Christian books. There are some excellent resources emerging. One I would recommend is *Our Bodies Tell God's Story* by Christopher West, which makes a compelling case from the structure of the human body itself for what appropriate sexual behavior is.

**Q: How can small churches build meaningful community when people drive from miles around and never connect outside Sunday?**

It has to arise from the intentional cultivation of friendships and communication beyond the Sunday service. That might take the form of congregational meetings, of informal gatherings during the week. Hospitality is a key component. Some of the most formative experiences students describe are not great lectures but the professors who invited them home for dinner. The church needs to be in the forefront of hospitality, particularly toward single people who often find themselves eating alone on Sundays.

**Q: Is the mental health community a potential ally for the church?**

The mental health community is more diverse than it gets credit for. Not all mental health professionals are on board with the transgender movement — those who are not simply tend to be silenced. When someone came to me showing signs of depression, I would always suggest they see a doctor as well, to determine whether there is something biological going on. Mental health professionals can serve the church. But pastors and churches are not just one voice among many in this conversation — the church has a distinctive and irreplaceable word to speak about what human beings are and what they are for.

**Q: What arguments might be made against the collapse of the concept of consent as a legal foundation?**

Consent is incredibly difficult to define. When there is a power differential between the parties, consent becomes very complicated. And consent is not unconditionally recognized in law for children: we do not require children to consent to their education, or to eating their vegetables. So child consent is already not an absolute legal principle. The argument that could be made against certain extensions of the logic of consent is empirical: that these things demonstrably harm children, harm people, harm the flourishing of society. That is the kind of argument I would make. Whether it will ultimately prevail, I do not know.

**Q: Is the conflict in contemporary culture best understood as a two-way fight between believers and unbelievers?**

I think it is actually a three-way fight: believers, humanists, and anti-humanists. On the question of human nature as basically good, humanists and anti-humanists will align against Christians. On the question of whether God exists, Christians and anti-humanists will disagree with humanists. On the question of morality — whether there are real moral truths that bind all people — Christians and humanists may find some common ground against the anti-humanists. The situation is more complex than a simple religious/secular binary, and recognizing that complexity may allow us to find unexpected allies on specific issues.
